questions.q: What are the two essential properties a problem must have for dynamic programming to apply?
options: Greedy choice property and optimal substructure
Divide and conquer structure and memoization
questions.explanation: Optimal substructure means an optimal solution to the problem contains optimal solutions to its subproblems. Overlapping subproblems means the same subproblems are solved repeatedly. DP exploits both: it stores subproblem results (memoization/tabulation) to avoid redundant computation.

questions.q: What is the time complexity of the classic 0/1 knapsack problem using dynamic programming?
questions.explanation: The DP table has n rows (items) and W+1 columns (capacities 0 to W). Filling each cell takes O(1), so the total time is O(nW). This is pseudo-polynomial — polynomial in n and W, but exponential in the number of bits needed to represent W.

questions.q: What is the difference between top-down and bottom-up dynamic programming?
options: Top-down uses iteration; bottom-up uses recursion
Top-down uses memoization with recursion; bottom-up fills a table iteratively
Top-down is always faster; bottom-up uses more space
They are the same approach with different names
questions.explanation: Top-down DP (memoization) starts from the original problem, recurses into subproblems, and caches results. Bottom-up DP starts from the smallest subproblems, fills a table in order, and builds up to the answer. Both have the same asymptotic complexity; bottom-up typically has less function-call overhead.
